question.text: Under the IDCW Re-investment option, what happens when an income distribution is declared by the scheme?

answer.text: The distribution amount is paid out to the investor's bank account
The existing units are split to reflect the distribution
The distribution amount is used to purchase additional units of the same scheme at ex-IDCW NAV
The distribution is automatically transferred to a liquid fund

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** In the IDCW Re-investment option, the declared distribution is not paid to the investor in cash. Instead, it is used to buy additional units of the same scheme at the prevailing ex-IDCW NAV. The investor ends up with more units but at a lower NAV per unit. The total investment value remains the same (pre-tax).
**The Trap:** IDCW Re-investment is NOT the same as Growth option. In Re-investment, the IDCW is first declared (triggering tax liability) and then reinvested. In Growth, no distribution is declared at all, so no tax event occurs until redemption.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Tax treatment differs significantly: IDCW Re-investment is taxed at the time of distribution (TDS applicable), while Growth option defers tax until actual redemption. This makes Growth option generally more tax-efficient for long-term investors.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The IDCW Re-investment option creates unnecessary tax events. Unless the client has specific reasons, the Growth option is usually preferable. Help clients understand that reinvestment does not mean tax-free compounding.
